tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

### Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The name stands for **Decentralized Operating System**. <cite index="10-3">DXOS is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

### Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="2-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="2-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="2-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="2-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="2-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **Organizational structure:** <cite index="10-4,10-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite>
- **Team:** <cite index="8-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite> <cite index="31-7">The community spans 8 cities worldwide, with 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.</cite>

### Mission & Vision

<cite index="2-1,2-2,2-3,2-4">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

<cite index="1-21,1-22">They are setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

### Core Technology Stack

DXOS consists of three main protocol layers:

1. **ECHO (Data Layer):** <cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite>

2. **HALO (Identity Layer):** <cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite>

3. **MESH (Network Layer):** <cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### Technical Architecture

- **CRDT-Based Sync:** <cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>
- **Cloudless Design:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **Networking:** <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- **Signaling:** <cite index="5-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>
- **Platform Support:** <cite index="4-5,4-6">DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.</cite>
- **Licensing:** <cite index="33-5">Open-source under the MIT license.</cite>

### SDKs & Developer Tools

- <cite index="6-4">The DXOS repository provides a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="4-27,4-28">DXOS recommends Vite as the bundler. Vite requires a plugin in order to serve the WebAssembly modules.</cite>

### Composer — The Flagship Application

<cite index="36-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

<cite index="16-7">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- <cite index="31-17">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-19">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-21">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-25">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-26">Embedded diagrams and sketches for programmatic workflows.</cite>
- <cite index="31-29">Machine learning for agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-36">Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>
- <cite index="31-51">Import and export of the entire database as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- <cite index="31-39,31-40">Everything inside of Composer is a plugin, meaning you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need.</cite>
- <cite index="35-7,35-8">Scripts in Composer allow you to deploy custom functions that run on the edge. Built-in voice/video meetings with real-time transcription, task extraction & semantic CRM enhancements.</cite>

**Privacy & Data Ownership:** <cite index="14-7,14-8">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite> <cite index="31-1,31-2">You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>

**Status:** <cite index="36-2,36-3">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite>

### Agents

<cite index="5-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite> <cite index="14-12,14-13">Agents are DXOS instances running without an attached user interface. A common use of agents is to run on a server and provide availability if all other devices are offline, but this is completely optional.</cite>

### Ecosystem Connections

- **Protocol Labs Network:** <cite index="30-3,30-4">Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.</cite>
- **BlueYard Capital:** DXOS is listed as a portfolio company of BlueYard Capital, a VC firm managing $500M in AuM that invests in pre-seed to Series A rounds.
- **Effect ecosystem:** <cite index="26-1">Jess Martin (DXOS) was among speakers at the first Effect conference in February 2024.</cite>

### Key Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org/
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org/
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org/
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer
- **Discord:** Community available via Discord

### Summary

DXOS represents a significant effort to build a viable, fully decentralized alternative to the traditional cloud-based SaaS model. By combining local-first storage (ECHO), decentralized identity (HALO), and peer-to-peer networking (MESH) into a cohesive framework with TypeScript and React SDKs, it aims to make it easy for developers to build collaborative applications where users retain ownership of their data. Its flagship product, Composer, serves as both a proof-of-concept and a practical productivity tool for teams, while the broader platform vision includes agents, extensible plugins, and AI integration — all operating without centralized server infrastructure.
